---
title: "Environmental Forcing and Predator Effects on Antarctic Krill (*Euphausia superba*) Population Dynamics: An Integrated Length-to-Age Stock Assessment Framework"
subtitle: "Version 2 — Revised"
author:
  - Mauricio Mardones ^[Universidad de Magallanes, mamardon@umag.cl; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile; Centro IDEAL, Chile; Instituto Español de Oceanografía, Spain]

  - Andrea Pinones ^[Universidad Austral de Chile; Centro IDEAL, Chile; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile]

  - Francisco Santa Cruz ^[Instituto Antártico Chileno, Universidad de Magallanes, Chile; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile]

linkcolor: blue

\newpage

```{r setup1, echo=FALSE}
rm(list = ls())
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval=FALSE,
                      echo = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      fig.align = 'center',
                      fig.pos = "H",
                      fig.show='hold',
                      dev = 'jpeg',
                      dpi = 300,
                      tidy.opts=list(width.cutoff=50),
                      tidy=TRUE)
```

```{r message=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
library(here)
library(kableExtra)
library(ggpubr)
library(tibble)
library(readxl)
library(openxlsx)
library(r4ss)
library(readr)
library(flextable)
library(officer)
library(r4ss)
```

\newpage

# Graphical abstract

```{r eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Trade-off between parsimony and realism in krill stock assessment models. Models that incorporate ecosystem drivers (environmental variability and predator effects) into the population dynamics are more ecologically realistic but may have poorer statistical performance than models that ignore these drivers."}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Figs", "parsimony_realism_tradeoff.svg"))
```


# Abstract

Understanding the population dynamics of Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*) is fundamental for ecosystem-based fisheries management in the Western Antarctic Peninsula, where environmental variability and predator–prey interactions are known to strongly influence population productivity. However, most stock assessment frameworks applied to krill and other exploited marine resources rely on model-based approaches in which ecosystem drivers are not explicitly embedded within the core population dynamics. These drivers are typically explored through external sensitivity analyses or post-hoc diagnostics, thereby limiting their influence on estimated stock status and management advice. In this study, a spatially implicit integrated length-to-age assessment model is applied to Antarctic krill, allowing ecosystem drivers to be incorporated directly into the population dynamics. Within a Stock Synthesis–based framework, alternative model configurations are evaluated in which environmental covariates and predator indices are either explicitly linked to key state processes (recruitment and natural mortality) or excluded from the structural equations. This design allows us to assess their effects on recruitment, total biomass, spawning stock biomass, and fishing mortality. Systematic and coherent differences are observed among model configurations. When environmental variability and predator effects are embedded within the state equations, lower estimates of recruitment, total biomass, and spawning biomass are obtained, together with altered estimates of fishing mortality, relative to models in which ecosystem drivers are ignored. In addition, poorer statistical performance is generally observed for these ecosystem-informed models, as indicated by higher RMSE values, more pronounced retrospective patterns, and more structured residual patterns. This deterioration in fit is interpreted as the result of the increased informational and structural cost incurred by the model when additional components are imposed, rather than as evidence against the relevance of the ecological mechanisms themselves. Overall, these results indicate that stock assessments excluding ecosystem processes may overestimate population productivity and stock status by prioritizing statistical fit over ecosystem realism. Despite the challenges associated with integrating ecosystem information into fully integrated assessment models, it is demonstrated that environmental and predator effects can be consistently incorporated within the core population dynamics of krill assessments. The direct embedding of these drivers in the state equations provides a more precautionary and ecologically realistic representation of krill population dynamics, and strengthens the scientific basis for management advice consistent with CCAMLR's objectives for conservation and sustainable use in the Western Antarctic Peninsula.

*Keywords: Antarctic krill, population dynamics, length-to-age model, stock-recruitment, ecosystem variables, model performance, fisheries management, CCAMLR*

\newpage

# Introduction

Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*, hereafter krill) constitutes one of the largest populations on the planet in terms of biomass and serves as a keystone species in the Antarctic ecosystem [@Atkinson2019a; @McBride2021; @Kawaguchi2024]. The population is predominantly concentrated in the Western Antarctic Peninsula (WAP), being the primary area for krill harvesting over the past four decades and also an area that is also a critical feeding ground for predators such as penguins, seals, and whales [@Watters2013; @Hill2016; @Gallagher2023a]. At the same time, the WAP is one of the regions most severely affected by climate change, experiencing rapid increases in ocean temperature, sea ice loss, and shifts in primary productivity [@Turner2005; @Lima2013; @Atkinson2019a; @Morley2020; @Carrasco2021; @McBride2021]. These overlapping pressures have intensified scientific interest in understanding krill population dynamics from both an ecological and a fisheries perspective in such a complex and rapidly changing environment [@Warwick-Evans2022; @Kawaguchi2024].

Given the importance of krill to the ecosystem and its commercial value, effective management of krill stocks is essential for maintaining the balance of the Antarctic ecosystem while supporting sustainable fisheries. Recognizing this, the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR) has been developing a revised ecosystem-based approach [@Constable2011; @Watters2013; @Cavanagh2016; @Constable2023; @Zhao2023; @Kawaguchi2024]. This strategy seeks to balance commercial harvesting with a precautionary framework that safeguards krill-dependent species across spatial and temporal scales. It comprises three key components: (i) stock assessments to establish precautionary harvest rates, (ii) periodic updates of krill biomass estimates at multiple spatial scales, and (iii) spatial overlap analysis to guide the allocation of catches and mitigate ecosystem-wide risks [@Warwick-Evans2022; @Kawaguchi2024].

To provide scientific advice on the status and standing stock of krill, CCAMLR has historically relied on dedicated scientific surveys [@Watters2013; @Hill2019; @Dornam2021; @Green2023]. However, changes in survey design—both methodological and spatial—have raised concerns about the reliability of these estimates, while the high costs associated with such surveys have further motivated the search for alternative stock estimation methods. More recently, and as part of its new strategy, CCAMLR has explored a variety of model-based (indirect) approaches to assess krill stock status and support fisheries management decisions [@Hill2019]. Some of these stock assessment models have used a single source of data—either fishery-dependent catches [@Wang2021] or survey-based monitoring programs [@Kinzey2011; @Kinzey2019; @Green2023]. At present, the Generalized Yield Model (GRYM) [@Maschette2020] is a model-based approach used by CCAMLR to generate management recommendations, allowing adjustments to the decision rules to predefined catch limits set by CCAMLR [@Constable2000; @CCAMLR2010; @CCAMLR2021a]. However, model-based approaches exhibit important limitations, including the effective integration of multiple data sources, the incorporation of ecosystem-level variables into population dynamics, and the capacity to account for the high spatial and temporal variability intrinsic to krill productivity [@Meyer2020; @Trathan2021].

Increasing evidence demonstrates that environmental conditions—such as sea surface temperature, sea ice extent, and phytoplankton availability—strongly influence krill recruitment, survival, and spatial distribution [@Murphy2007; @Flores2012; @Saba2014; @Morley2020; @Perry2020; @Walsh2020; @Vorrath2020; @Veytia2021]. Similarly, fluctuations in predator abundance and distribution can exert significant top-down control on krill populations [@Moosa2017; @Reisinger2022; @Baines2022; @Riaz2023; @Biuw2024]. There is now broad scientific consensus on the need for stock assessment models that incorporate multiple data sources, capture temporal and spatial variability, and explicitly account for ecosystem interactions [@Marshall2019]. Neglecting these ecosystem drivers can lead to biased estimates of stock status and suboptimal management advice [@Haltuch2009; @Richards2016; @Marshall2019; @Crone2019; @Robertson2024]. Stock assessment frameworks aligned with CCAMLR's new strategy should therefore explicitly incorporate these ecosystem components to more accurately represent krill population processes.

Integrating ecological complexity into krill stock assessments presents multiple challenges. Long-term data collection is often affected by changes in monitoring programs and sampling methodologies [@Hill2019], while some key regions of the WAP lack sufficient data to construct reliable krill population indices. Additional difficulties arise from indirect drivers—such as environmental variability and predator–prey interactions—which are difficult to quantify due to spatial and temporal heterogeneity, non-linear responses in krill dynamics, and the simultaneous influence of multiple stressors [@Constable2023]. Despite these challenges, substantial data exist on krill and their associated environments. Fishery monitoring programs provide detailed biological data from catches through onboard scientific observers, notably through CCAMLR's Scheme of International Scientific Observation (SISO), which ensures standardized and independent biological sampling of the krill fishery across the Convention Area. Multiple biomass assessment surveys based on hydroacoustic cruises are also conducted by CCAMLR, primarily led by the United States and China, whose programs represent the longest-running efforts and the broadest spatial coverage of krill hydroacoustic assessments in the Southern Ocean [@Hill2019; @Dornam2021; @Green2023]. Ecosystem monitoring programs, such as the CCAMLR Ecosystem Monitoring Program (CEMP), track predator populations and environmental variability at key sites. Together, these initiatives support ecosystem-based management by offering insights into both direct fishery impacts and the broader ecological context influencing krill dynamics. This diversity of data creates an opportunity to develop integrated assessment frameworks that account for the coupled biological, ecological, and fishery processes shaping krill populations.

CCAMLR has traditionally relied on assessments conducted within a data-limited context using relatively simple model-based approaches, such as GRYM models, which do not explicitly incorporate environmental or ecological variables in the representation of population dynamics. In contrast, a wide range of integrated assessment frameworks now allow the incorporation of diverse data sources and increasing levels of structural complexity. These include spatially explicit approaches such as the State-Space Assessment Model (SAM) [@Nielsen2021] and the Globally Applicable Area-Disaggregated General Ecosystem Toolbox (Gadget) [@Bartolino2011]. For statistical catch-at-age assessments, widely used frameworks include the C++ Algorithmic Stock Assessment Laboratory (CASAL) [@Bull2004] and Stock Synthesis (SS3) [@Methot2013], among others. Notably, Stock Synthesis can also be applied to species that are difficult to age, such as Antarctic krill, through integrated length-to-age transformation models that explicitly link observed length compositions to underlying age-structured population dynamics. These advanced tools provide robust and flexible methodologies to address data limitations while accommodating complex biological processes and ecosystem interactions, thereby enabling a more realistic representation of population dynamics and improved scientific support for ecosystem-based fisheries management [@Hoyle2020].

This study pursues two main objectives. The first is to integrate multiple data sources on Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*)—including fishery operations (e.g., catches and effort distribution), biological characteristics (e.g., growth rates and reproductive parameters), spatiotemporal population dynamics, and key ecosystem drivers (e.g., environmental forcing and predator–prey interactions)—into a unified stock assessment framework. Specifically, we apply an integrated length-to-age model that enables the explicit inclusion of environmental covariates affecting biomass, recruitment patterns, and overall productivity, as well as the potential influence of predator populations through the configuration of natural mortality. The second objective is to evaluate the performance of alternative model configurations representing different hypotheses regarding ecosystem influences, using diagnostic tools and performance metrics commonly applied in contemporary stock assessments. Consistent with CCAMLR's ecosystem-based management framework [@Constable2011; @Cavanagh2016; @Hill2019; @Constable2023; @Zhao2023; @CCAMLRWGSAM2024; @WGEMM2024], this approach provides empirical evidence of the value of ecosystem-informed assessment models, demonstrating that the incorporation of environmental and predator covariates influences population variables, model fit, predictive skill, and structural uncertainty—with implications for precautionary and sustainable management advice for the Antarctic krill fishery.

\newpage

# Methodology

## Study Area

The study area covers the WAP, corresponding to FAO Statistical Subarea 48.1, one of the main regions for krill fishing activity and where CCAMLR is actively developing a revised management approach at a finer spatial scale. To enhance the spatial resolution of our analysis of krill population dynamics, five Management Units (MUs) proposed by CCAMLR were used: Bransfield Strait, Elephant Island, Gerlache Strait, Joinville Island, and the Southwest sector [@Dornam2021] (S1 Fig. 1). Although these management units were not originally designed to incorporate krill biological or population-specific characteristics [@Watters2013; @Constable2023; @Zaldua2024a], their subdivision allows for finer-scale examination of krill data, enhancing spatial resolution and facilitating the identification of heterogeneity among different data sources—including fishery-dependent and environmental information—thereby supporting a more detailed characterization of krill population dynamics.


## Spatial Implicit Model

To represent spatial heterogeneity in krill dynamics, we implemented a spatially implicit modeling approach based on the "areas-as-fleets" framework [@Waterhouse2014; @Punt2019; @Nielsen2021]. This approach treats distinct geographical regions as independent fleets, under the assumption that fishing and survey operations may have varying effects across space. Within this approach, each MU of the WAP is treated as a distinct fleet, allowing spatial differentiation in key parameters such as selectivity, catchability (*q*), and abundance indices. This is especially relevant for krill given the documented spatial variability and heterogeneity in both population processes and fishing effort [@Perry2019; @Perry2020; @Reiss2020; @Veytia2021; @SantaCruz2022; @Mardones2026]. By modeling each MU as a separate fleet, the assessment can estimate region-specific parameters that reflect this variability. Survey indices and biological compositions (e.g., length-frequency data) are assigned to their corresponding MU based on sampling location as defined by [@Dornam2021]. Similarly, fishery-dependent data (such as catch and size composition) are stratified by MU according to reported fishing effort, allowing for regional variation in both abundance and observational processes. Given known differences in population structure [@Kinzey2011; @Reiss2020; @Cutter2022], selectivity is modeled separately for each MU to account for potential variations in size composition due to spatial heterogeneity or environmental conditions. This disaggregated structure allows the model to reflect small-scale spatial dynamics while maintaining analytical simplicity. The "areas-as-fleets" approach effectively incorporates regional variability in both population dynamics and observational processes without the added complexity of explicitly modeling movement [@Punt2019]. The model accounts for interannual variability in growth, maturity, and reproduction, assuming a single annual spawning pulse occurring between January and February [@Perry2019; @McBride2021]. Although MUs are treated as separate fleets, core population processes—such as recruitment and growth—are modeled collectively, representing the krill stock as a single interconnected population, which provides a more realistic representation of krill population dynamics within Subarea 48.1.


## Length-to-Age Model Description

Like many invertebrates, krill present challenges for age determination, which complicates modeling population dynamics. Most modern stock assessments rely on age-structured modeling approaches to estimate marine population parameters [@Punt2013]. Although recent advances have improved krill aging techniques [@Kilada2017], such methods have historically been unavailable for krill. Consequently, stock assessment for krill typically uses length data to approximate age structure through transition matrices. These matrices estimate the probability that individuals of a given length belong to specific age classes [@Laslett2004; @Lee2019; @Rudd2021; @Zhang2022; @Lee2024]. Given the scarcity or absence of direct age data in krill monitoring programs, length-based models offer a practical alternative to age-structured approaches. To date, all model-based krill assessments have used length-to-age frameworks [@Kinzey2015; @Kinzey2019; @Wang2021; @Green2023; @Zhang2022]. Integrated models can effectively capture age structure by transforming length observations into population-level dynamics [@Punt2013; @Lee2024]. Length data, which are cost-effective and readily available [@Chong2019; @Canales2021], also provide valuable insights into krill population structure due to their correlation with age [@Thanassekos2014].

To perform this transformation, models typically use Age-Length Keys (ALKs). An ALK is a probabilistic matrix that estimates the likelihood of individuals of a given length belonging to specific age classes. This method assumes that both aged and measured individuals are random samples from the same population and should be applied within the same time period to avoid biases [@ICCA2003; @Punt2003; @Punt2013; @Lee2024]. Using this statistical framework, we constructed the ALK for Antarctic krill, which was subsequently applied within the assessment model (S1 Fig. 3). In the catch-at-length stock assessment model, growth was parameterized using a von Bertalanffy growth function (VBGF) for length-at-age. This formulation was integrated with the weight-at-length relationship, which was assumed known and derived from the parameters reported by @Maschette2020 for Antarctic krill. To account for early ontogeny, linear growth was assumed below a predefined settlement age of 1.5 years. Maturity was modeled as an age-based logistic function, with the onset of maturity occurring between 2 and 3 years [@Thanassekos2014]. Fishery selectivity was represented by a logistic function defined by the size at 50% selectivity ($L_{50}$) and the selection range ($L_{95}$). The estimated inflection points ranged from 2.0 to 3.5 cm, while the widths spanned from 2.0 to 4.0 cm across fleets or MUs. Survey selectivity was also modeled using a logistic formulation, with inflection points between 2.0 and 3.0 cm. These differences in selectivity parameters are critical for interpreting spatial heterogeneity among MUs, as they reflect variability in size-dependent catchability across fleets (S1 Fig. 5).

The stock–recruitment (SR) relationship was modeled using a Beverton–Holt function, following @Kinzey2019. The steepness parameter was fixed at 0.85 to maintain consistency with previous studies, reflecting the weak relationship between spawning biomass and recruitment in krill populations, where recruitment is largely driven by environmental variability [@Dong2025; @Kinzey2019; @Perry2020]. Unfished recruitment ($R_0$) was estimated assuming a mean recruitment of 23 mm (log-scale), with a prior of 10. Recruitment deviations were included to capture interannual variability using the deviation vector approach (*recdev = 1*), whereby recruitment is modeled as a function of spawning stock biomass plus a deviation term. Cyclic recruitment patterns were also incorporated, assuming periodicity at three- and five-year intervals [@Perry2019; @McBride2021; @Kinzey2019; @Thanassekos2014]. All life-history parameters and assumptions were specified as priors to define the initial population dynamics, based on data from @Smith2023a, @Maschette2020, and @Kinzey2011. These biological parameters, together with the initial selectivity assumptions, are summarized in Table 1 S1.

The statistical model for krill was configured using Stock Synthesis v3.30.21 (SS3) [@Methot2013]. SS3 is a structured stock assessment model belonging to the class of integrated stock assessment models, with a set of sub-models that simulate growth, maturity, fecundity, recruitment, and mortality processes, along with observation sub-models that generate expected values for different data types. The model is coded in Automatic Differentiation Model Builder (ADMB) [@Fournier2012; @Methot2013; @methot2020stock] with estimated parameters. Model conditioning is available in the code repository section of Supporting Information 1. All modeling analyses were conducted in R [@R-base], with visualization and diagnostics performed was made through *r4ss* [@Taylor2019] and *ss3diags* [@Winker2023] packages.

## Environmental Link Formulation

Within the Stock Synthesis (SS3) framework, environmental covariates influence population dynamics through link functions applied to underlying model parameters (e.g., recruitment, natural mortality, or growth), rather than acting directly on observed quantities [@methot2020stock; @Caserta2025]. These parameter-level effects propagate to derived quantities such as annual recruitment. Let $P_t$ denote a generic model parameter influenced by the environment, $P_{base}$ its baseline value under average environmental conditions, $E_t$ the environmental covariate, and $\beta_E$ the estimable coefficient describing environmental sensitivity. Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) was selected as the environmental covariate based on its well-documented influence on krill population dynamics in the Western Antarctic Peninsula [@Flores2012; @Morley2020; @Veytia2021; @Walsh2020], and its demonstrated effect on krill recruitment productivity specifically confirmed by @Saba2014 and @Mardones2025. Chl-a was obtained from a combination of satellite products including *Nimbus-7* and the *EU Copernicus Marine Service*. Prior to model fitting, the Chl-a time series was standardised to zero mean and unit variance:

$$E_t = \frac{C_t - \bar{C}}{\sigma_C}$$

where $C_t$ is the raw annual Chl-a concentration (mg m$^{-3}$), and $\bar{C}$ and $\sigma_C$ are the historical mean and standard deviation. This standardisation improves numerical stability, renders $\beta_E$ interpretable as the change in the target parameter per one standard deviation anomaly in Chl-a, and is consistent with recommended practice in SS3 [@methot2020stock]. Under this formulation, the environmental link captures interannual variability in recruitment relative to the long-term baseline $\ln(R_0)_{base}$. The environmental effect was implemented via an additive link (link type 2 in SS3) [@Caserta2025]:

$$P_t = P_{\text{base}} + \beta_E \cdot E_t$$

where $\beta_E$ is estimated freely within the model. For years with missing Chl-a observations, SS3 assigns $E_t = 0$, effectively reverting the parameter to its baseline value [@methot2020stock].

## Predator Modelling

Predation is incorporated in SS3 through an additional mortality component ($M_2$), augmenting background natural mortality ($M_1$) [@methot2020stock]. This approach enables partial representation of ecosystem processes within a single-species framework, in contrast to fully dynamic ecosystem models such as ATLANTIS or Ecopath with Ecosim [@fulton2003; @fulton2011; @christensen2004]. Total instantaneous mortality is defined as:

$$Z_{a,t} = M1_{a,t} + \sum_i M2_{a,t,i} + F_{a,t}$$

where $M2_{a,t,i}$ represents predation mortality from predator $i$. In our krill assessment, predation pressure was represented using the three main penguin species in the Antarctic Peninsula: Adélie (*Pygoscelis adeliae*), Chinstrap (*Pygoscelis antarcticus*), and Gentoo (*Pygoscelis papua*), whose annual abundance was obtained from 399 colonies monitored over the period 2000–2020 through the Mapping Application for Penguin Populations and Projected Dynamics ([MAPPPD](https://www.penguinmap.com/mapppd/sources/)) [@Oceanites2021]. Penguin abundance was aggregated across species and used as a combined predator index. Length-frequency distributions of krill from penguin stomach contents, used to characterise predator size selectivity, were obtained from the U.S.AMLR Scientific Research Program (AMLR), with data available from 1992 onwards. The predator component was implemented as a dedicated fleet within SS3, combining two observational data streams: (i) the aggregated penguin abundance index, used to inform time-varying predation mortality, and (ii) the annual krill length-frequency distributions from stomach contents, used to estimate predator selectivity. Predation mortality was parameterized as:

$$M2_{a,t} = Q \times I_{pred,t}$$

where $I_{pred,t}$ is the standardized predator index and $Q$ is an estimable scaling parameter, analogous to catchability in fishing mortality [@methot2020stock]. The term $M2_{a,t}$ was further parameterised as a time-varying quantity via annual log-scale deviations with a zero-reverting logit-link random walk (dev\_link = 5 in SS3), which constrains $M2_{a,t}$ within biologically plausible bounds across the time series. Diet composition data, in the form of annual length-frequency distributions of krill from penguin stomach contents, informed the size-selectivity function of predation. A two-parameter logistic selectivity was used:

$$S(l) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\ln(19) \cdot (l - \mu) / \delta}}$$

where $\mu$ is the length at 50% selectivity and $\delta$ is the 95% width of the ascending limb. Parameters were estimated within SS3 using the stomach content length data as the observational likelihood component for the predator fleet, allowing the model to reconcile abundance and diet data streams during estimation [@methot2020stock]. Model diagnostics for predation focus on the relative contribution of predation to total mortality, summarized by the ratio $M2/Z$, as well as the shape of the estimated selectivity function, which reveals size-dependent predation patterns. The M2 framework relies on simplifying assumptions, including the absence of predator–prey feedbacks, a linear functional response between predator abundance and predation mortality, and the use of population averaged selectivity.

## Data Sources

Along with data on predators and environmental conditions described previously, a primary input to the model was derived from fishery-dependent data and scientific monitoring programs. Fishery-dependent data were obtained from the CCAMLR Krill Fishery Monitoring Program and the SISO Scheme, including total catches in weight, effort (grouped by vessel, area, and depth), and krill length-frequency distributions. Length data were standardized using a fixed bin structure of 28 length intervals, each representing the lower edge of 0.2 cm width bins from 1.2 to 6.6 cm. This ensured consistency across years, MUs, and fleets (fishery, survey, and predator), facilitating reliable temporal and spatial comparisons of size structure, covering the period from 1990 to 2020. Fishery-independent data originated from U.S. AMLR Program acoustic surveys and included standardized biomass indices conducted between 1991 and 2011.

Fishery-independent data originated from U.S. AMLR Program acoustic surveys and included standardized biomass indices conducted between 1997 and 2011 by MU [@Dornam2021]. The discontinuation of biological sampling after 2011 reflects the transition of the AMLR monitoring programme towards autonomous underwater vehicle glider-based acoustic surveys [Reiss2021]. Consequently, length-frequency data from this source are unavailable for the most recent period of the time series. However, this limitation does not substantially compromise the assessment, as the biological data spanning 1991–2011 encompass the full range of observed environmental and fishing conditions, providing sufficient temporal coverage to reliably estimate growth, selectivity, and size-structure dynamics that are propagated into subsequent model years through the population dynamics equations. Krill length compositions from AMLR surveys were processed following the same format as fishery-dependent data and handled according to stock assessment model template requirements (Suppl. Mat. 2). All data, together with the previously described ecosystem variables (environmental and predator-related), their temporal coverage, and their spatial scale, are summarized in Table \@ref(tab:data-sources) and illustrated in S1, Fig. 4.


```{r data-sources, eval=TRUE}
data <- data.frame(
  Source = c(
    "Krill Fishery Monitoring Program (CCAMLR SISO)",
    "Krill Fishery Monitoring Program (CCAMLR SISO)",
    "Krill Fishery Monitoring Program (CCAMLR SISO)",
    "AMLR Scientific Research Program",
    "Satellite Remote Sensing",
    "Predator Monitoring (MAPPPD)",
    "Predator Diet Composition (AMLR)"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Total reported catches",
    "Standardized CPUE (by vessel, area, and depth)",
    "Length-frequency distributions (0.2 cm bins)",
    "Biomass indices by MU from net sampling",
    "Monthly chlorophyll-a concentration from EU Copernicus Marine Service (interpolated; doi:10.48670/moi-00021, doi:10.48670/moi-00148)",
    "Abundance by colony-specific trends of penguin populations",
    "Length-frequency of krill in predator diets"
  ),
  TemporalScale = c(
    "1997–2020",
    "1997–2020",
    "2000–2020",
    "1991–2011 (based on cruise periods)",
    "1997–present",
    "2000s–present",
    "1977–2020 (subset from 2000 used in this analysis)"
  ),
  SpatialScale = c(
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "all Subarea 48.1",
    "By colony",
    "By species"
  )
)

# to word
ft_data <- flextable(data) %>%
  set_caption("Table: Data sources used in the krill population dynamics modeling") %>%
  theme_vanilla() %>%
  fontsize(size = 9, part = "all") %>%
  font(fontname = "Arial", part = "all") %>%
  autofit() %>%
  set_table_properties(width = .5, layout = "autofit")

ft_data
```

\newpage

## Scenarios

A set of model configurations was evaluated, incorporating variations in predator "fleet" and environmental effects, and their impacts on spawning biomass, recruitment, and other population variables. The methodology involves assessing the statistical performance of the model under these configurations.  Specifically, `s1.1` serves as the reference model, incorporating only fishery and survey data while accounting for spatial heterogeneity (implicit spatial model). The subsequent scenarios build upon this configuration to evaluate the effects of additional ecological components. `s1.2` extends `s1.1` by incorporating predator influences, allowing the model to assess the impact of predation on krill population dynamics. `s1.3` modifies `s1.1` by introducing an environmental variable, capturing potential environmental drivers of recruitment. Finally, `s1.4` integrates both predator and environmental influences into the `s1.1` framework, providing a more complete representation of ecosystem interactions and their role in krill population dynamics. Regarding spatial heterogeneity, this approach was considered in all scenarios (Table \@ref(tab:scenario)).

```{r scenario, eval=TRUE}
scenario_table <- data.frame(
  Scenario    = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
  Description = c(
    "Base model",
    "Base model + predator mortality",
    "Base model + environmental covariate",
    "Base model + predator mortality + environmental covariate"
  ),
  Predator    = c("No", "Yes", "No",  "Yes"),
  Environment = c("No", "No",  "Yes", "Yes"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

ft_scenarios <- flextable(scenario_table) |>
  set_header_labels(
    Scenario    = "Scenario",
    Description = "Description",
    Predator    = "Predator (M\u2082)",
    Environment = "Chl-a link"
  ) |>
  set_caption("Table X. Model configurations evaluated for Antarctic krill stock assessment.") |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") |>
  bold(part = "header") |>
  align(j = c("Predator", "Environment"), align = "center", part = "all") |>
  autofit() |>
  set_table_properties(width = 1, layout = "autofit")

ft_scenarios
```

Regarding performance evaluation and scenario comparison, @Carvalho2017, @Carvalho2021b, @Merino2022a, and @Kell2021 propose a set of best practices for conducting a structured diagnostic assessment to ensure the reliability and robustness of stock assessment analyses. This diagnostic process was applied to the krill stock assessment and involved several key steps. First, convergence analysis was conducted to evaluate whether the estimation algorithm reached a stable solution. This included reviewing parameter trace plots, gradients, and the Hessian matrix to ensure that all parameters were well-estimated. Next, model fit to data was assessed by comparing observed and predicted values across key data sources, including catch, survey indices, and length composition. The *p*-values for the residual runs tests were computed for each of the four joint length compositions common to all models. The *p*-values were then combined into a single test statistic using Fisher's method [@Merino2022a]:

$$
\chi^2_{2k} = -2 \sum \log(p_i)
$$

where \( p_i \) is the p-value for length composition \( i \) and \( k \) denotes the degrees of freedom corresponding to the four *p*-values. Likelihood profiling was also carried out to quantify parameter uncertainty and identify the influence of priors. Pearson residuals of predicted length distributions for krill were evaluated across the four modeling scenarios to assess goodness-of-fit by standardizing the differences between observed and predicted values, allowing for the detection of systematic patterns or biases in model predictions across size classes and sampling sources. Hindcast cross-validation is another metric to measure perfomances model and evaluates model predictive skill by comparing one-step-ahead hindcast predictions against observed data using the Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE), which scales the model's mean absolute error relative to that of a naïve random-walk baseline. A MASE > 1 indicates that the model predicts no better than a random walk; MASE < 1 indicates genuine predictive skill; and MASE = 1 denotes equivalence with the naïve baseline. Retrospective analysis and hindcast cross-validation were performed by sequentially removing five years of data following @HurtadoF2015. As part of the retrospective analysis, the Mohn parameter was calculated as the mean relative error, defined as:


> A MASE score greater than 1 indicates that the model’s average forecasts are less accurate than a random walk model.
- A MASE score equal to 1 suggests that the model’s accuracy is similar to that of a random walk.
- A MASE score less than 1 indicates that the model performs better than a random walk.
- A MASE score of 0.5, for example, indicates that the model’s forecasts are twice as accurate as the naïve baseline prediction, suggesting the model has predictive skill.


$$
\rho_M = \frac{1}{h} \sum_{y=1}^{h} \left( \frac{X_{T - y} - \hat{X}_{T - y}}{\hat{X}_{T - y}} \right)
$$

where \( X \) represents the quantities of interest (such as spawning stock biomass, SSB, or fishing mortality, F), \( \hat{X} \) is the corresponding estimate from the reference model fitted to the full dataset, \( T \) refers to the terminal assessment year, and \( h \) denotes the number of retrospective peels. This procedure involves removing recent years of data sequentially and re-estimating the model, allowing calculation of retrospective bias (*Rho*) for key metrics such as SSB and F. To evaluate statistical differences in model performance, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) values were extracted from the variance tuning diagnostics of each scenario and compiled for statistical analysis. To test for overall differences among scenarios, a one-way ANOVA [@Sthle1989] was conducted, treating RMSE values as the dependent variable and scenario configurations as the categorical factor. Model performance was also evaluated using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) [@Bozdogan1987], which balances model fit and complexity, facilitating comparisons across scenarios. Lower AIC values indicate more parsimonious models with better overall fit. The ACF of annual recruitment deviations was used to assess residual temporal structure in the estimated recruitment series across model configurations [@jhonson2016]. 

Table \@ref(tab:metrics) summarizes the diagnostic metrics used to evaluate model performance, including goodness-of-fit, prediction accuracy, information criteria, retrospective bias, and estimation stability.

```{r metrics, eval=TRUE}
df_model_metrics <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "Pearson Residuals",
    "Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)",
    "Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE)",
    "Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)",
    "Rho Parameter (B and SSB)",
    "ACF of Recruitment Deviations"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Standardized residuals calculated as the difference between observed and expected values, divided by the standard deviation of the expected values. Useful for assessing model fit across data points.",
    "Measures the average magnitude of the residuals (prediction errors), giving higher weight to large errors. Lower values indicate better predictive accuracy.",
    "A scale-independent measure of prediction accuracy. It compares forecast errors to the errors from a naive baseline model. Values <1 indicate better-than-baseline performance.",
    "A relative measure of model quality that balances model fit and complexity. Lower AIC values indicate better models, penalizing overfitting.",
    "The autoregressive correlation coefficient from retrospective analysis, used to assess systematic bias in biomass (B) and spawning stock biomass (SSB) estimates across years.",
    "Autocorrelation function applied to annual recruitment deviations (RecDevs). Significant autocorrelation at successive lags indicates residual temporal structure not captured by the model, suggesting misspecification of recruitment dynamics."
  ),
  Reference = c(
    NA,
    "@Carvalho2021b",
    "@Hyndman2006",
    "@Bozdogan1987",
    "@HurtadoF2015",
    "@jhonson2016"
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

kbl(df_model_metrics, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE,
    caption = "Summary of model diagnostic metrics used for krill stock assessment") |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down", "striped"),
                font_size = 9) |>
  column_spec(1, width = "3.5cm", latex_valign = "m") |>
  column_spec(2, width = "9cm", latex_valign = "m") |>
  column_spec(3, width = "3.5cm", latex_valign = "m")

```
\pagebreak

# Results

## Model Performance and Complexity Across Scenarios

Scenario `s1.1` represented the baseline spatial implicit model, incorporating only survey and fishery data, and resulted in the estimation of 71 parameters. In scenario `s1.2`, predator-related components were added—including biomass and size-selective predation patterns from key penguin colonies—increasing the parameter count to 107. Scenario `s1.3` introduced an environmental covariate as a recruitment driver into the baseline model, leading to the estimation of 96 parameters. Finally, scenario `s1.4` integrated both predator and environmental components, culminating in the highest model complexity with 128 estimated parameters. All model scenarios successfully converged within acceptable tolerance thresholds, with final gradients below 1.00e-04 and positive-definite Hessian matrices, confirming robust parameter estimation and local identifiability.

Pearson residuals from length composition data were examined as an initial diagnostic of model fit across all scenarios (S1, F x). All four scenarios produced residuals broadly centered around zero across fleets, years, and length bins, consistent with adequate convergence and absence of systematic directional bias. No scenario exhibited patterns of persistent over- or underestimation severe enough to question the overall model structure. In scenario s1.1, residuals across fishery and survey fleets are generally small and unstructured, with sporadic deviations concentrated in the larger size classes of certain survey fleets, particularly those operating in the Bransfield Strait. This baseline configuration captures the central tendency of observed length distributions reasonably well, though some residual signal remains unexplained in the tails of the size distribution. Scenario s1.3, which adds the environmental link to the baseline, shows a residual pattern closely resembling that of s1.1 across all shared fleets, reflecting the limited structural influence of the standardised environmental covariate on the fitted size compositions under this parameterisation. Scenarios s1.2 and s1.4, which incorporate the predator fleet, additionally show residuals associated with penguin stomach content length distributions. In s1.2, the predator fleet exhibits moderate to large residuals in certain years and size classes, suggesting that predation mortality alone introduces tension between the predator data and the remaining data streams. In s1.4, predator fleet residuals appear somewhat more balanced, with a more even distribution across years and size bins, tentatively suggesting that the joint inclusion of environmental and predator components provides a marginally more coherent fit to the full dataset. This pattern warrants further examination through complementary diagnostics.

RMSE patterns differed between data types and revealed a consistent trade-off across model configurations (Figure \@ref(fig:rmseplot)). For abundance indices, scenarios incorporating predator-derived natural mortality (s1.2, s1.4) achieved lower median RMSE and reduced interquartile ranges relative to the base model (s1.1) and the environmental-only configuration (s1.3), indicating improved fit to survey and fishery abundance trends. The opposite pattern was observed for length compositions, where s1.1 and s1.3 produced lower median RMSE and narrower dispersion than s1.2 and s1.4, suggesting that the additional structural complexity introduced by the predator term increases residual variability in size-structured fits. 

```{r rmseplot, eval=TRUE, fig.cap="Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) distributions across four model scenarios for abundance indices (left) and length compositions (right). Lower values indicate better model fit. Points represent fleet-specific RMSE values."}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/rmse_scenarios.png')
```

Hindcast cross-validation revealed consistent differences in predictive performance across configurations (Figure \@ref(fig:maseplt)). Scenario s1.2, incorporating predator-derived natural mortality, achieved the lowest mean MASE across all indices (2.11), followed by s1.4 (2.29), which combines predator mortality with the environmental covariate. The base model (s1.1; mean MASE = 2.72) and the environment-only configuration (s1.3; mean MASE = 2.74) showed the poorest predictive performance and were virtually indistinguishable from each other. Across all scenarios, mean MASE values exceeded 1, indicating that no configuration achieved systematic predictive skill superior to a random walk at the aggregate level. However, this result was strongly influenced by FISHERYGS, which consistently returned the highest individual MASE values (~8–9) across all scenarios, disproportionately inflating scenario-level means regardless of model structure. In contrast, acoustic survey indices (SURVEYEI, SURVEYGS) were the only components achieving MASE < 1 across all configurations, suggesting these data streams carry genuine one-step-ahead predictive signal.

```{r maseplt, eval=TRUE,  out.width="50%", fig.cap="Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) for krill population variables under scenarios of increasing ecosystem complexity"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/mase_hcxval_scenarios.png')
```

The retrospective pattern for fishing mortality (F) showed a marked increase in bias in scenarios `s1.2` and `s1.4`, which included predator components, particularly in 2017 (Figure \@ref(fig:rhoplot)). In `s1.4`, the combined rho value exceeded 3.5, indicating a strong retrospective pattern. Scenarios `s1.2` (predator only) and `s1.3` (environment only) also showed elevated rho values relative to `s1.1` (baseline model without ecosystem variables), although the magnitude was lower than in `s1.4`. In contrast, the retrospective pattern for spawning stock biomass (SSB) was consistently negative across all scenarios and years. Models that included environmental components—`s1.3` and `s1.4`—tended to show larger negative biases in SSB, with `s1.2` and `s1.4` again exhibiting the most pronounced patterns. The combined rho values for SSB in `s1.3` and `s1.4` were close to −0.5 and −0.6, respectively, whereas `s1.1` showed a comparatively milder retrospective trend.


Retrospective analysis revealed contrasting patterns across scenarios and quantities (Figure \@ref(fig:rhoplot). For fishing mortality (F), scenario s1.1 showed a combined Mohn's $\rho$ close to zero, indicating limited systematic bias across peels. Scenario s1.3 also showed stable F estimates. Scenarios s1.2 and s1.4 exhibited more pronounced retrospective patterns, with s1.4 producing an extreme positive $\rho$ in the 2017 peel ($\rho$ > 3). For spawning stock biomass (SSB), s1.1 again showed the lowest combined retrospective bias. Scenario s1.3 produced the most extreme SSB $\rho$ across individual peels, exceeding 3 in the 2016 peel. Scenarios s1.2 and s1.4 showed moderate negative SSB $\rho$ values across peels, with s1.4 reaching approximately $-$0.75 in the 2017 peel. Combined Mohn's $\rho$ values across all scenarios and quantities are summarised in Table \@ref(tab:diagnostics). The full retrospective trajectories for SSB and F across all peels and scenarios are provided in Supplementary Material (Figures SX and SX, respectively).


Retrospective analysis revealed divergent and in some cases extreme patterns across scenarios and quantities (Figure \@ref(fig:rhoplot). For spawning stock biomass (SSB), s1.1 and s1.3 showed positive combined Mohn's $\rho$ (0.119 and 0.446, respectively), indicating a systematic tendency toward overestimation when data from recent years are progressively removed. Scenario s1.3 was particularly notable, with individual peel deviations exceeding 3 in the 2016 peel, suggesting that the environmental covariate introduces structural instability in SSB estimation under data reduction. Scenarios s1.2 and s1.4 produced negative combined SSB $\rho$ ($-$0.220 and $-$0.629), reflecting retrospective underestimation, with s1.4 reaching approximately $-$0.75 in the 2017 peel. For fishing mortality (F), s1.1, s1.2, and s1.3 all showed negative combined $\rho$ values (ranging from $-$0.869 to $-$0.954), indicating a consistent tendency to overestimate F in terminal years relative to earlier peels, though without extreme individual deviations. Scenario s1.4 departed markedly from this pattern, producing a strongly positive combined $\rho$ of 1.754 and an extreme spike in the 2017 peel ($\rho$ > 3), indicating severe retrospective instability in F when predator mortality and environmental covariates are incorporated simultaneously. This spike suggests that the 2017 peel year is a leverage point for the joint ecosystem configuration, likely reflecting the sensitivity of F estimation to the interaction between predator-driven mortality and environmental recruitment signals in a data-limited peel context. The full retrospective trajectories for SSB and F are provided in Supplementary Material (Figures SX and SX, respectively).

```{r rhoplot, eval=TRUE, out.width="75%", fig.cap="Retrospective patterns for fishing mortality (F, left) and spawning stock biomass (SSB, right) across four modeling scenarios differing in ecosystem complexity. Each point represents the rho value for a given terminal year (2016–2019), with Combined indicating the average retrospective bias across years in different scenarios"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/rhoplot.png')
```

The autocorrelation of annual recruitment deviations was used to assess residual temporal structure in the estimated recruitment series across model configurations (Figure \@ref(fig:acfrecruit)) . Scenarios s1.1 and s1.3 exhibited recruitment deviations largely uncorrelated across lags, with all autocorrelation coefficients remaining within the 95% confidence bounds. Scenario s1.2 showed moderate autocorrelation at intermediate lags, without systematic exceedance of significance thresholds. Scenario s1.4 displayed the highest degree of autocorrelation, with the coefficient at lag 1 exceeding the significance threshold and several subsequent lags exhibiting notable structure.

```{r acfrecruit, eval=TRUE, fig.cap="Autocorrelation function (ACF) of recruitment deviations for four krill population model scenarios. Blue dashed lines indicate 95% confidence intervals"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/ACF_recruitment.png')
```

The stock-recruitment relationship (SRR) for Antarctic krill, estimated using the  Beverton-Holt model (Equation X), revealed consistent and biologically coherent differences across model configurations (S1, Fig X. A). Scenarios excluding predator-derived natural mortality (s1.1, s1.3) produced substantiallyhigher asymptotic recruitment curves (~40–45 billion individuals), with predicted 
values remaining elevated even at low spawning biomass levels, suggesting high compensatory productivity under these assumptions. In contrast, configurations incorporating the M2 predator term (s1.2, s1.4) yielded markedly lower and more saturating SRR curves (~10–15 billion), implying reduced population productivity when predation mortality is explicitly accounted for. This contrast is reinforced in panel B (S1, Fig X. B), which shows recruitment efficiency (R/SSB) as a function of relative spawning biomass (SSB/SSB~0~): s1.1 and s1.3 consistently exhibit higher per-capita productivity across all depletion levels, while s1.2 and s1.4 show substantially lower efficiency, particularly at low SSB. The virgin recruitment parameter ln(R~0~) (S1, Fig X. C) confirms this grouping: s1.1 and s1.3 estimate higher unfished recruitment (~24.3–24.5), whereas s1.2 and s1.4 converge on lower values (~23.4), with s1.4 showing the most precise estimate (narrowest posterior).


All four scenarios achieved full convergence, with maximum gradient components below $1 \times 10^{-4}$ (Table \@ref(tab:diagnostics)). Model complexity increased progressively from s1.1 (96 parameters) to s1.4 (104 parameters), reflecting the incremental addition of ecosystem covariates. Based on AIC, s1.3 provided the best overall parsimony-adjusted fit (1,631.4), closely followed by s1.1 (1,636.9), while s1.2 (2,531.8) and s1.4 (2,655.1) showed substantially higher values, indicating that the addition of predator-derived mortality incurred a considerable informational cost not offset by improved fit.

Examination of likelihood components revealed divergent patterns depending on the data type. Total and length composition likelihoods were markedly lower in s1.1 and s1.3 (394.1 and 393.1, respectively) than in s1.2 and s1.4 (~908–949), indicating substantially poorer fit to length frequency data in predator-inclusive configurations. In contrast, survey likelihood was lowest in s1.2 (290.2) and s1.4 (306.8), outperforming s1.1 (362.0) and s1.3 (346.8), suggesting that predator scenarios achieved better alignment with abundance index trends despite their overall poorer fit. RMSE for abundance indices confirmed this pattern: s1.2 (72.5%) and s1.4 (74.6%) outperformed s1.1 (77.1%) and s1.3 (76.1%), whereas RMSE for length composition was lowest in s1.1 and s1.3 (9.1%) and highest in s1.4 (10.0%). Predictive skill as measured by MASE was best in s1.2 (2.114) and s1.4 (2.286), and poorest in s1.3 (2.735) and s1.1 (2.719), indicating that predator-inclusive configurations yielded superior one-step-ahead forecast performance despite their worse length composition fit.

```{r diagnostics, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
diagnostics <- read_csv("outputs/model_diagnosis_results.csv")
grad_row <- which(diagnostics$Description == "Final gradient")
param_row <- which(diagnostics$Description == "No. parameters")

flextable(diagnostics) |>
  colformat_num(j      = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
                digits = 2,
                na_str = "-") |>
  compose(
    i     = grad_row,
    j     = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
    value = as_paragraph("<1.00e-04")
  ) |>
  compose(
    i     = param_row,
    j     = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
    value = as_paragraph(
      as_chunk(format(as.integer(diagnostics[param_row, c("s1.1","s1.2","s1.3","s1.4")]),
                      big.mark = ""))
    )
  ) |>
  set_header_labels(Description = "Metric") |>
  set_caption("Statistical components to evaluate performance and diagnostics of model scenarios") |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") |>
  bold(part = "header") |>
  autofit() |>
  set_table_properties(width = 1, layout = "autofit")
```

## Comparison between scenarios

Krill population variables showed consistent temporal trends across all scenarios over the period 1989–2020, differing primarily in magnitude rather than in the direction or timing of population dynamics (Figure \@ref(fig:compara1)). Three biomass metrics are reported alongside age-0 recruitment: total biomass ($B_{all}$), which includes all individuals at the start of each year; summary biomass ($B_{smry}$), which excludes individuals below the minimum summary age and represents the fraction vulnerable to surveys and the fishery; and spawning stock biomass (SSB), computed at the time of peak spawning (austral summer). SSB may exceed $B_{smry}$ in years of rapid somatic growth between the start of the year and the spawning event, consistent with the fast growth rates characteristic of Antarctic krill [@Atkinson2006].

All four scenarios tracked the same interannual dynamics: a period of relatively stable biomass from 1989 to approximately 2010, followed by a marked increase peaking around 2015–2016, and a partial decline thereafter. Recruitment (age-0) showed high interannual variability across all scenarios, consistent with the episodic and environmentally driven nature of krill recruitment [@Dornam2021], with a pronounced peak in 2015 shared by all configurations. These common trends indicate that the underlying population signal is robust across model structures. Differences among scenarios were, however, substantial in magnitude: configurations excluding predator-derived natural mortality (s1.1, s1.3) consistently produced higher estimates across all variables, with biomass and recruitment peaks approximately two to five times greater than those estimated under s1.2 and s1.4. The most constrained estimates were observed under s1.4, where predator mortality and environmental covariates operate simultaneously on both recruitment and natural mortality.


```{r compara1,eval=TRUE,  out.width="100%", fig.cap="Time series of population variables of krill in WAP: total biomass, summary biomass, (C) recruitment, and (D) spawning biomass"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/popvar_scenarios.png')
```

Boxplot distributions of population indicators across the assessment period 1989–2020 are shown in Supplementary Figure X. Scenarios excluding predator-derived natural mortality (s1.1, s1.3) consistently produced higher median estimates and wider interquartile ranges for total biomass, summary biomass, and recruitment relative to scenarios incorporating the M2 predator term (s1.2, s1.4). This grouping was most evident in recruitment, where s1.1 and s1.3 showed medians approximately two to three times higher than s1.2 and s1.4. Spawning stock biomass followed a similar pattern, with s1.1 yielding the highest median and s1.2 and s1.4 the lowest. Scenario s1.4 exhibited the most constrained recruitment distribution (narrowest interquartile range), reflecting the combined effect of predator mortality and environmental covariates on population productivity.

Model outputs under different ecosystem scenarios revealed substantial variation in krill population indicators, including total biomass, summary biomass, recruitment, and spawning biomass (S1, Fig X). The reference model (`s1.1`), which excluded environmental and predator components, consistently produced higher median estimates and wider uncertainty intervals across all population indicators. Inclusion of predator components (`s1.2`) or environmental variables (`s1.3`) led to reductions in central tendency and variability for all metrics, with `s1.3` (environment-only) showing the most constrained estimates, particularly for recruitment and spawning biomass. Scenario `s1.4`, which incorporated both environmental and predator components, resulted in intermediate biomass and recruitment estimates with moderate variance compared to the other scenarios. Recruitment was especially sensitive to the inclusion of ecosystem components. The highest median recruitment was observed under `s1.2` (predator-only), while the lowest was seen in `s1.3` (environment-only), highlighting the contrasting influences of these ecological factors.





<!-- # Discusion -->

<!-- This study advances understanding of Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*) population dynamics by addressing two fundamental objectives. First, it consolidates and harmonizes multiple long-term datasets collected over decades—particularly from Subarea 48.1 in the Western Antarctic Peninsula—into a unified statistical framework, responding to a recurrent management priority regarding integrated models for krill emphasized by CCAMLR. Second, it integrates ecosystem variables such as environmental drivers and predator impacts, thereby moving beyond the traditional single-stock approach and taking a step toward ecosystem-informed stock assessments. This approach not only enhances the biological realism of krill population modeling but also reveals the structural complexity and statistical challenges that must be addressed for its effective application in Antarctic fishery management contexts. -->


<!-- ### Spatial Considerations and the Areas-as-Fleets Approach -->

<!-- Recognizing and accounting for spatial heterogeneity in krill populations across the large expanse of Subarea 48.1 was essential, particularly for determining how to incorporate this dimension into the integrated model. To address this, the areas-as-fleets approach was applied, a methodology that captures spatial differences and incorporates them into global estimates of population variables. This framework allows for a more realistic representation of local variability in abundance, selectivity, and population processes [@Nielsen2021; @Waterhouse2014]. However, some MUs—particularly Joinville Island, with few years of survey and fishery monitoring data—had limited data. While these data limitations were not explicitly evaluated here, they could introduce complications in future integrated krill modeling efforts and should be carefully considered in subsequent analyses. -->

<!-- ### Environmental Drivers and Recruitment Dynamics -->

<!-- The identification of Chl-a concentration as the primary environmental driver affecting krill recruitment highlights the central role of primary productivity in shaping krill population dynamics [@Ainley2017; @Constable2023; @Saba2014]. This finding is consistent with recent studies emphasizing that krill recruitment is primarily driven by environmental conditions rather than spawning stock biomass [@ichii2023; @Perry2020; @Veytia2021]. Models incorporating environmental variables (`s1.3` and `s1.4`) showed significantly lower AIC values, indicating better model performance and parsimony compared to models without environmental data. While integrating environmental variables introduces complexity to model structure and performance, it also enhances model realism—a key challenge currently faced by stock assessment approaches. By explicitly incorporating variables that are mechanistically linked to key population processes such as recruitment, these models move beyond purely stochastic representations of stock productivity [@Haltuch2009; @Crone2019], allowing for a more process-informed understanding of population variability and improving both explanatory power and predictive capacity. -->

<!-- ### Predator–Prey Interactions and Natural Mortality -->

<!-- The integration of penguin predation data into natural mortality estimates represents a novel approach in an integrated stock assessment, and even more so for krill. This size-selective predation pattern is biologically consistent with foraging theory and observed predator behavior; no significant differences in krill size ranges were identified among key *Pygoscelis* predator species in their diet [@Panasiuk2024]. The declining trend in the synoptic predator biomass index, particularly after 2015, reflects documented population declines in Antarctic Peninsula penguin colonies [@Emmerson2022; @Kruger2019; @Salmeron2023; @Trivelpiece2011a]. These declines, primarily affecting Adélie and Chinstrap penguin populations, have been attributed to climate change impacts, sea ice loss, and shifts in prey availability [@Kruger2019]. The incorporation of this declining predation pressure into the stock assessment model provides a mechanism for capturing ecosystem-mediated changes in natural mortality that would otherwise be overlooked in traditional single-species approaches. -->

<!-- Scenarios with explicit inclusion of predator components (`s1.2` and `s1.4`) showed increased bias in estimates of fishing mortality and SSB, and yielded lower estimates of recruitment, total biomass, and spawning biomass. Incorporating predation mortality into stock assessment models is essential for accurately representing population dynamics in marine ecosystems and for providing a more precautionary and ecologically realistic representation of krill dynamics. Efforts to incorporate predation as a modeling component in marine stock assessments have precedent in the literature. @Hollowed2000 revealed higher total natural mortality rates than traditional life-history estimates and showed that ignoring predation uncertainty underestimated stock biomass uncertainty by up to 20%. Similarly, @Richards2016 introduced a predation pressure index for northern shrimp, combining predator biomass and diet frequency to scale natural mortality over time. This method improved model fit and reduced retrospective bias without requiring absolute predator consumption data. For Antarctic krill stock assessments, incorporating predation variability improves mortality estimates and supports ecosystem-based management. -->

<!-- The synoptic index of relative predator biomass accounts for variations in colony abundance, providing a robust metric that reflects the overall status of the penguin population (bottom panel in Figure \@ref(fig:mapapre)). -->
<!-- This decline has been extensively documented in the Antarctic Peninsula by studies such as [@Waterhouse2014; @Dunn2019; @Wethington2023], primarily associated with Adélie and Chinstrap penguin colonies [@Strycker2020; @Talis2023]. This abundance series captures broad decadal declines in predator presence, particularly after 2015, potentially reflecting changes in prey availability, environmental conditions, or predator demography. -->

<!-- ### Model Performance and Diagnostic Evaluation -->

<!-- Increasing model complexity can destabilize internal matrices and estimation processes, potentially leading to convergence issues [@Kinzey2015; @Kinzey2019; @Dong2025]. While all models in this study successfully converged and produced Hessian matrices suitable for quantifying parameter uncertainty, the comprehensive diagnostic evaluation revealed important trade-offs between biological and ecological realism and statistical performance. Although the ecosystem-informed models (particularly `s1.4`) showed improved biological realism through reduced Pearson residuals and better representation of length-frequency distributions, they also exhibited increased retrospective bias and reduced likelihood values compared to the baseline model (`s1.1`). The elevated retrospective bias in fishing mortality estimates for ecosystem scenarios—particularly the pronounced bias (rho = 6.912) in scenario `s1.4`—suggests potential overfitting or structural instability when both environmental and predator variables are simultaneously incorporated. The observed bias patterns may also reflect temporal mismatches between ecosystem variables and population responses, suggesting that lagged effects or non-linear relationships should be explored in future model development. -->

<!-- The shift toward ecosystem-based fisheries management highlights the need for stock assessment models that explicitly incorporate species interactions, environmental drivers, and predator pressures. For Antarctic krill, integrating ecosystem variables—such as predation pressure and environmental variability—can improve the realism of population dynamics; however, it also increases model complexity, which in turn affects model fit and raises parameter uncertainty, revealing a trade-off between traditional single-species assessments and ecosystem-informed approaches, as described by @Collie2016 and @Robertson2024. This study embraced that challenge and confirmed the inherent difficulties, while demonstrating that this approach represents a step in the right direction, offering valuable insights for the evolving management strategy that CCAMLR seeks to implement for the krill fishery. -->


<!-- Pearson -->

<!-- Residual misfits concentrated among larger size classes in several Bransfield Strait survey fleets suggest that the current model structure may not fully resolve the processes governing the abundance and selectivity of adult krill in this sub-region. A plausible explanation is spatial mismatch: the model operates under spatial implicit assumptions, which may inadequately represent the distinct size-structured distribution of krill across management units. The Bransfield Strait has been consistently identified as a core juvenile habitat [@Mardones2025; @Veytia2021; @Perry2020; @Perry2019], implying that adult individuals are disproportionately distributed elsewhere, and that survey selectivity in this area may be biased towards smaller size classes. Incorporating explicit spatial structure into future assessments could reduce these residual patterns by allowing size-selective processes to vary across strata. -->

<!-- Similarly, persistent residuals in the largest size classes of the predator fleet point to potential limitations in how size-selective predation is represented. Penguins are known to exhibit prey size preferences that vary seasonally and interannually in response to krill availability [@Marin2001; @Silvestro2023], yet the current model applies a time-invariant logistic selectivity function. This simplification may inadequately capture temporal shifts in predation pressure across the krill size spectrum, contributing to systematic deviations in the predator fleet residuals. -->

<!-- Taken together, these residual patterns reflect the inherent cost of imposing ecosystem structure within a spatially implicit single-species framework. Rather than indicating fundamental model failure, they identify specific processes, spatial heterogeneity in size structure and temporally dynamic predation selectivity, that represent natural targets for future model development. -->

<!-- Autocorrelated RecDevs indicate that a model has failed to fully capture the underlying drivers of year-to-year recruitment variability, leaving unexplained signal that propagates across consecutive years [@Thorson2014; @jhonson2016]. This diagnostic is particularly relevant for Antarctic krill, whose recruitment is strongly modulated by environmental forcing that can sustain conditions favorable or unfavorable to larval survival over multiple consecutive years [@Flores2012; @Veytia2021]. The absence of significant autocorrelation in s1.1 and s1.3 suggests that these configurations adequately absorbed interannual recruitment variability without generating systematic temporal residuals. The pronounced autocorrelation observed in s1.4, by contrast, is consistent with a model that is overparameterised relative to the information content of the available data: when both $M_2(t)$ and $\beta_E E_t$ are estimated jointly, collinearity between ecosystem drivers prevents the optimiser from cleanly partitioning mortality and recruitment signals, forcing RecDevs to compensate for unresolved variance. This pattern does not reflect intrinsically greater autocorrelation in krill recruitment under predation pressure, but rather the inability of the combined model to simultaneously resolve both ecosystem effects, reinforcing the interpretation that s1.4 incurs a higher informational cost and poorer internal consistency than simpler configurations. -->


<!-- Retro -->
<!-- The deterioration in retrospective performance in ecosystem-informed scenarios reflects the informational cost incurred when predator and environmental drivers are embedded directly within the state equations of the assessment model. The instability of F estimates in s1.4 is consistent with identifiability constraints arising from the joint estimation of time-varying predation and fishing mortality from the same population signal. The extreme SSB rho observed in s1.3 reinforces the finding that the environmental link, as currently parameterised, introduces instability into biomass estimates without providing a stabilising ecological constraint. These patterns should not be interpreted as evidence against the ecological relevance of the drivers incorporated, but rather as a consequence of imposing structural constraints on a data-limited system, a trade-off central to the evaluation presented here. -->


<!-- MASE -->

<!-- The hindcast cross-validation (HCxval) diagnostic, implemented as a one-step-ahead prediction skill test, provides a robust framework for comparing model configurations beyond in-sample goodness-of-fit metrics (Carvalho et al., 2021⚠️). The Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE), originally proposed by Hyndman & Koehler (2006) as a scale-independent forecast accuracy metric, has been increasingly adopted in fisheries stock assessment to evaluate whether model predictions outperform a naïve random walk baseline. In this context, MASE values exceeding 1.0 across all scenarios — including the base model — reflect the extreme interannual variability inherent in Antarctic krill abundance, which is well documented in the literature (Atkinson et al., 2004⚠️; Everson, 2000⚠️) and represents a fundamental challenge for any deterministic assessment framework operating in this ecosystem. The persistent poor predictive performance of the FISHERYGS index (MASE ≈ 8–9 across all configurations) is consistent with the known spatial aggregation behaviour of krill, which generates high variability in fishery-dependent indices that is difficult to capture through model structure alone (Everson, 2000⚠️; Constable et al., 2000⚠️). -->
<!-- The relative improvement in predictive skill observed in configurations incorporating predator-derived natural mortality (s1.2, s1.4) suggests that temporally structured variation in M2, informed by penguin abundance indices from MAPPPD, introduces ecologically relevant information that partially improves the model's ability to track observed abundance trends. This is consistent with the role of top-down predator control in structuring krill population dynamics at sub-seasonal to interannual timescales in the Western Antarctic Peninsula (Trivelpiece et al., 2011⚠️; Constable et al., 2000⚠️). In contrast, the chlorophyll-a environmental link (s1.3) provided no detectable improvement over the base model, a result attributable to the structural properties of the additive Z-scored link rather than to the ecological irrelevance of primary productivity as a recruitment driver. This distinction between structural implementation and ecological mechanism is critical: the absence of predictive gain in s1.3 does not preclude a genuine relationship between chlorophyll-a and krill recruitment, but rather indicates that the current parameterisation does not translate that relationship into improved predictive capacity. Similar limitations have been noted in ecosystem-informed stock assessments where environmental covariates are incorporated additively without sufficient contrast in the covariate time series to generate identifiable recruitment effects (Punt et al., 2014⚠️). -->


<!-- The diagnostic results reveal a fundamental tension between statistical fit to compositional data and predictive skill for abundance indices. Scenarios s1.1 and s1.3 achieved the best AIC and length composition fit, yet performed worst in terms of MASE and survey RMSE — precisely the metrics that reflect predictive capacity for the primary biomass signals used by CCAMLR. This pattern is consistent with the risk of overfitting compositional data at the expense of capturing the broader population trajectory [@Punt2013; @Maunder2013]. Conversely, predator-inclusive scenarios (s1.2, s1.4) sacrificed fit to length compositions — which are abundant but potentially noisy data in krill assessment contexts [@Kinzey2019] — in exchange for better alignment with survey indices and improved forecast skill. The substantially higher length composition likelihoods in s1.2 and s1.4 likely reflect the structural tension between a predator-driven mortality signal and the observed size structure, suggesting that the M2 formulation imposes demographic constraints not fully reconcilable with the empirical length frequencies under the current selectivity parameterization. The extreme retrospective instability in fishing mortality under s1.4 (Mohn's $\rho$ = 1.754) warrants particular caution: while this configuration achieved the best survey RMSE, the F estimate is highly sensitive to the removal of recent data, indicating that the joint effect of predator mortality and environmental recruitment covariates creates leverage on terminal-year F that may compromise management advice reliability [@HurtadoF2015]. -->


<!-- ## Comparasion- -->

<!-- The systematic divergence between predator-inclusive and predator-exclusive configurations is consistent with the well-documented role of seabird and marine mammal predation as a major source of krill mortality in the Western Antarctic Peninsula (WAP). Predator consumption of krill in this region has been estimated to substantially exceed commercial catch removals, and its omission from population models is expected to produce upward-biased biomass estimates [@Murphy2007; @Constable2000]. The grouping pattern observed here — in which the presence or absence of M2 predator mortality, rather than environmental covariates, determines the level of estimated biomass — is consistent with findings from integrated ecosystem assessments showing that top-down predation pressure exerts a stronger and more continuous influence on krill population size than bottom-up environmental variability, which tends to operate episodically through recruitment [@Atkinson2004]. -->
<!-- The pronounced biomass and recruitment peaks estimated for 2015–2016 across all scenarios are consistent with anomalously favourable environmental conditions documented for this period in the WAP, including above-average chlorophyll-a concentrations and sea-ice dynamics conducive to krill spawning success [@Siegel2005]. The fact that these peaks are amplified in s1.1 and s1.3 suggests that, in the absence of predator mortality, the model attributes the full observed signal in survey indices to population growth rather than partitioning it between biological production and predation-driven removal. This confounding effect has practical implications for reference point estimation: biomass trajectories that do not account for predation will tend to overstate stock productivity and recovery potential, potentially leading to overly optimistic assessments of stock status relative to CCAMLR conservation objectives [@Constable2000; @Kinzey2019]. -->

<!-- Survey-aggregated biomass observations (blue diamonds; 95% CI propagated across management units assuming spatial independence) are overlaid on the $B_{smry}$ panel because SS3 calibrates survey catchability $q$ against this metric, such that $\ln(\hat{I}_t) = \ln(q) + \ln(B_{smry,t})$. The apparent discrepancy between raw survey totals and model-estimated $B_{smry}$ is expected: estimated $q > 1$ ($\ln q = 1.1$; $q \approx 3.0$) indicates that surveys sample areas of above-average krill density relative to the stock-wide mean [@Everson2000; @Siegel2005], and the sum of survey observations across management units represents a spatially biased index rather than an absolute stock estimate. This scaling is resolved internally within the Stock Synthesis likelihood framework. -->


<!-- ### Implications for Precautionary Management -->

<!-- While there are concerns about the performance of models incorporating ecosystem variables, their estimates of key population variables—including spawning biomass, total biomass, and recruitment—are generally lower than those produced by models without ecosystem variables (S1). The significant reduction in spawning biomass estimates between scenarios `s1.1` and `s1.4` (mean difference: 2.6 million tonnes) demonstrates that neglecting ecosystem variables may lead to overly optimistic stock assessments and potentially unsustainable harvest recommendations.
